topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. A crisp, formal notification stating that my piece on urban gardening, the one I’d poured months of late nights into, had been published under the name of a complete stranger. My hands began to tremble, the cheap plastic pen I was gripping slipping from my fingers. I reread the text, the unfamiliar author's voice sounding alien and mocking. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, squeezing the air from my lungs. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my apartment, the floorboards groaning under my hurried steps. Every shadow in the room seemed to shift, coalescing into something menacing. I kept glancing at the locked door, half-expecting someone to materialize in the hallway.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in my throat. The words swam before my eyes—my analysis, my passion, now belonging to someone else entirely. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision blur at the edges. I pressed my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block out the reality unfolding on the monitor. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I backed away from the desk, bumping into the bookshelf, sending a cascade of unrelated novels tumbling to the floor. Every sound in the quiet room—the distant hum of traffic, the tick of the clock—seemed amplified, pressing in on me. I needed to move, to escape the suffocating space where this deception had taken root.

[story 3]

When I finally opened the published article, the realization hit with brutal clarity. The byline was wrong. It was a ghost of my own work, lifted and repurposed. A sudden chill traced its way down my spine, settling deep in my bones. I sat down heavily on the edge of my bed, pulling my knees up to my chest, wrapping my arms tightly around myself. My knuckles turned white as I clenched my fists. I kept running my thumb over the rough fabric of my jeans, a repetitive, anxious motion. The silence of the room felt heavy, charged with an unspoken threat. I couldn't seem to draw a full breath; the air felt too thin, too dangerous to inhale.

A crisp, formal notification stating that my piece on urban gardening, the one I’d poured months of late nights into, had been published under the name of a complete stranger. My hands began to tremble, the cheap plastic pen I was gripping slipping from my fingers. I reread the text, the unfamiliar author's voice sounding alien and mocking. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision blur at the edges. I pressed my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block out the reality unfolding on the monitor. When I finally opened the published article, the realization hit with brutal clarity. The byline was wrong. It was a ghost of my own work, lifted and repurposed. A sudden chill traced its way down my spine, settling deep in my bones. I sat down heavily on the edge of my bed, pulling my knees up to my chest, wrapping my arms tightly around myself. My knuckles turned white as I clenched my fists. I kept running my thumb over the rough fabric of my jeans, a repetitive, anxious motion.
